questions.question: Which command-line parameter is used to perform a silent installation of Notion for Windows?

options.text: /i
options.explanation: The /i flag is not the standard parameter used by Notion's installer for silent installs.

options.text: /S
options.explanation: The /S flag is the standard parameter for the Nullsoft Scriptable Install System used by Notion to run a silent installation.

options.text: /q
options.explanation: While /q is a common silent install flag in other tools, Notion specifically uses /S.

options.text: /silent
options.explanation: Notion does not support the /silent flag.

questions.question: What happens immediately after a silent installation completes using the /S flag?

options.text: It launches the Notion application automatically.
options.explanation: The documentation explicitly states that once the installation has finished, it will not launch itself.

options.text: It prompts the user to enter a license key.
options.explanation: Silent installations bypass user interface prompts, including license key entry.

options.text: It displays a success message in a popup window.
options.explanation: Silent installations run without showing any user interface.

options.text: It does not launch itself.
options.explanation: This is the correct behavior defined for the /S flag.

questions.question: In PowerShell, why is it recommended to pipe the installation command output to Out-Null?

options.text: To reduce memory usage of the system.
options.explanation: While Out-Null reduces output stream usage, its primary purpose here is to control script flow.

options.text: To ensure the script waits for the installation process to finish.
options.explanation: PowerShell by default continues to the next command immediately. Piping to Out-Null forces PowerShell to wait for the external process to complete before proceeding.

options.text: To delete the installer executable file after installation.
options.explanation: Out-Null simply discards the standard output stream; it does not delete files.

options.text: To hide errors from the log file.
options.explanation: Out-Null suppresses output to the screen, but it does not specifically target error handling.

questions.question: Which parameter is used to specify a custom installation directory?

options.text: /P
options.explanation: /P is not a valid parameter for directory specification in Notion's installer.

options.text: /path
options.explanation: /path is not a valid parameter for directory specification.

options.text: /D
options.explanation: The /D parameter is used to specify a custom directory.

options.text: /C
options.explanation: /C is not a valid parameter for directory specification.

questions.question: When using the /D parameter for a custom directory, what must the path be?

options.text: A relative path (e.g., ../CustomPath)
options.explanation: The path must be an absolute path, not a relative one.

options.text: A UNC network path (e.g., \\server\share)
options.explanation: The documentation specifies that the path must be absolute; while UNC paths are absolute, the standard examples shown are local drive paths.

options.text: An absolute path (e.g., C:\CustomPath)
options.explanation: The /D parameter requires an absolute path.

options.text: A path within the user's AppData folder.
options.explanation: There is no restriction that the path must be within AppData.

questions.question: Which of the following is a requirement regarding the /D parameter?

options.text: The path must be enclosed in quotes.
options.explanation: There should be no quotes around the path, even if it contains spaces.

options.text: It must be the last parameter in the command.
options.explanation: This is a strict requirement: the /D parameter must be the last parameter.

options.text: It supports relative paths (e.g., Folder/Subfolder).
options.explanation: It only supports absolute paths.

options.text: It can only be used in conjunction with the /S flag.
options.explanation: The /D parameter can be used independently or with other flags, but it must be the last one.

questions.question: Why do users need read and write permissions for the custom installation directory?

options.text: Because the installer needs to write logs to the location.
options.explanation: While the installer writes files, the auto-updater specifically requires write access.

options.text: Because the auto-updater attempts to write to this path.
options.explanation: The documentation explicitly notes that the auto-updater will attempt to write files to the custom path.

options.text: Because the OS requires write access for desktop shortcuts.
options.explanation: Desktop shortcuts are typically created in the AppData or Start Menu, not necessarily the custom install path.

options.text: Because the installer requires write access to verify the path exists.
options.explanation: Verification happens during install, but the ongoing requirement is for the auto-updater.

questions.question: How should you format the path in the /D parameter if it contains spaces?

options.text: Use single quotes around the path.
options.explanation: Single quotes are not valid in standard command-line syntax for this parameter.

options.text: Use double quotes around the path.
options.explanation: Double quotes are not allowed; the path should have no quotes at all.

options.text: Do not use any quotes around the path.
options.explanation: The documentation explicitly states there should be no quotes around the path, even if spaces are present.

options.text: Replace spaces with underscores.
options.explanation: You do not need to modify the path structure; just ensure quotes are not used.

questions.question: Is it currently possible to disable automatic updates for Notion for Windows?

options.text: Yes, by using a specific command line flag.
options.explanation: There is no known method to disable automatic updates via command line or settings.

options.text: Yes, but only for Enterprise users.
options.explanation: The article indicates this feature is not available at all for Windows users.

options.text: No, it is not currently possible.
options.explanation: The Help Center article explicitly states that automatic updates cannot be disabled at this time.

options.text: Yes, by editing the Windows Registry.
options.explanation: There is no mention of registry manipulation or disabling updates in the provided documentation.
